mission.title: kubearmor: securing KubeArmor pods and policies (Policing the Police!)
mission.description: securing KubeArmor pods and policies (Policing the Police!). Community-requested feature.

steps.title: Check current kubearmor deployment
steps.description:
Verify your kubearmor version and configuration:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n kubearmor -l app.kubernetes.io/name=kubearmor
helm list -n kubearmor 2>/dev/null || echo "Not installed via Helm"
```
This feature requires a working kubearmor installation.

steps.title: Review kubearmor configuration
steps.description:
Inspect the relevant kubearmor configuration:
```bash
kubectl get all -n kubearmor -l app.kubernetes.io/name=kubearmor
kubectl get configmap -n kubearmor -l app.kubernetes.io/part-of=kubearmor
```
KubeArmor is a security engine and thus it is imperative that it follows all the security best practices. The aim is to ensure security of the KubeArmor itself.

steps.title: Apply the fix for securing KubeArmor pods and policies (Policing the Police!)
steps.description:
- How would KubeArmor daemonset attach a profile to itself?
  - Failed admission if annotation added and no profile found

Potential Solution
- Snitch has the intelligence to load the default apparmor profile.

See the source issue for community-verified solutions.

steps.title: Verify the feature works
steps.description:
Test that the new capability is working as expected:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n kubearmor -l app.kubernetes.io/name=kubearmor
kubectl get events -n kubearmor --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' | tail -10
```
Confirm the feature described in "securing KubeArmor pods and policies (Policing the Police!)" is functioning correctly.

resolution.summary:
- How would KubeArmor daemonset attach a profile to itself?
  - Failed admission if annotation added and no profile found

Potential Solution
- Snitch has the intelligence to load the default apparmor profile.

prerequisites.description: A running Kubernetes cluster with kubearmor installed or the issue environment reproducible.
